summary: Add the `type` field primitive over the closed scalar/container lattice — `Predicate::Type`, parsed and decided against the preserved source kind.

per.section: Decision: the `type` vocabulary is a closed scalar/container lattice

edit.description: Give `Kind` a string⇄kind mapping: parse a declared type name (`string`/`integer`/`number`/`boolean`/`list`/`map`/`null`) into a `Kind`, and render a `Kind` back to that name for diagnostics. This is the home of the lattice (TYPED-EXTRACTION), so the name table lives here, not duplicated in the contract parser. Test: each lattice name round-trips; an unknown name yields `None`.

Add `Predicate::Type { field, kind }` to the closed `Predicate` vocabulary (`kind: extract::Kind`); add a `type` arm to `parse_predicate` that reads `field` plus a `type` key and maps it through the `extract.rs` name table — an unknown declared type is a load error (a new `ContractError` variant, mirroring how `UnknownPredicate` rejects an out-of-vocabulary key). Tests: a `type` clause parses into the lattice; an unknown declared type is a load error.

Add the `Predicate::Type` arm to both exhaustive matches: `decide` (compare the field's preserved source kind — `features.field(field).map(FeatureValue::kind)` — to the declared kind; an absent field is the `required` clause's concern, so `type` stays silent on absence) and `predicate_key` (returns `"type"`). Tests: fires on a kind mismatch, silent on a match and on an absent field.

tests.asserts: each lattice name maps to its `Kind` and back; an unknown type name is rejected
a `type` clause parses into the closed lattice and rejects an unknown declared type at load
`type` fires exactly when a field's source kind differs from the declared kind, silent on match/absence

acceptance: a `type` clause parses over the closed lattice, rejects an unknown type at load, and fires exactly when a field's preserved source kind differs from the declared kind.
notes: Closed lattice only (string/integer/number/boolean/list/map/null) — no formats, unions, nested schemas, or numeric ranges; those are the JSON-Schema unsound-proxy surface the bespoke-closed-algebra Decision rejects. `min_len`/`max_len`/`enum`/`pattern` refine within a scalar type; `type` only fixes the kind. Admissibility needs no new arm — `type` carries no list, so `inadmissibilities`' catch-all covers it.
